explicit_text: I was born and raised in Ethiopia, specifically in the city of Nazret. Ethiopia is a beautiful country with diverse landscapes, and my family and I lived there before I moved abroad. My parents, Nasir Ahmed and Shetu Mohammed, along with my siblings, still live there. Leaving Ethiopia in 2007 was difficult because I had to leave my close-knit family and culture behind.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a country in the Horn of Africa, specifically in the city of Nazret. This nation is beautiful with diverse landscapes, and my family and I lived there before I moved abroad. My parents, Nasir Ahmed and Shetu Mohammed, along with my siblings, still live there. Leaving that East African country in 2007 was difficult because I had to leave my close-knit family and culture behind.

explicit_text: My family belongs to the Gurage ethnic group in Ethiopia. Growing up, I learned a lot about Gurage culture from my father and aunt, especially about our traditions and foods. The Gurage people have unique marriage customs, like the way boys formally request a girl's hand. Our family is very close, and the Gurage language and traditions have always been a big part of my identity.

explicit_text: Nazret, also called Adama, is where I grew up. It’s a city in central Ethiopia, southeast of Addis Ababa, surrounded by mountains and changing colors with the seasons. I have many fond memories of living there with my family on the same property as my brother. Nazret was a beautiful place that shaped much of my early life.

explicit_text: Addis Ababa is the capital city of Ethiopia and is located northwest of my hometown Nazret. While I didn’t live there, it’s an important reference point for where Nazret is situated. Addis Ababa represents the larger urban center near my roots and the country I come from.

explicit_text: In 2007, I traveled to Qatar and stayed there for four years before moving to the United States. My time in Qatar was a significant transition period away from Ethiopia and my family. It was an important step in my journey before settling in America.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in 2011 after living in Qatar. My journey in the U.S. started with a brief stay in Ohio, then I moved to Dallas, Texas, and now I live in Minnesota. The U.S. has been my home for over a decade, but I still carry my Ethiopian and Gurage heritage with me.

explicit_text: Currently, I live in Minnesota, where I have been since leaving Dallas. Minnesota has become my new home in the United States. Even though I am far from Ethiopia, I keep my culture alive here.

explicit_text: My father’s name is Nasir Ahmed, and he still lives in Ethiopia with my mother and siblings. I am very close to him; he often shared stories about his family and loved to joke. His stories helped me understand our Gurage culture and kept me connected to my roots even when I was far away.

explicit_text: Kitfo is one of the traditional Gurage foods I always remember from my childhood in Ethiopia. My family would gather together to eat kitfo along with other dishes during celebrations and family times. It’s a dish that brings back warm memories of home and togetherness.

explicit_text: Kocho is another traditional food from my Gurage heritage that I grew up eating. It was always part of our family meals and celebrations back in Ethiopia. Kocho reminds me of the close family bonds and cultural traditions we shared.

explicit_text: Gomen, a traditional Ethiopian dish, was often served at family gatherings in my Gurage household. Eating gomen with my family was a joyful experience that connected me to my culture. I miss those times and the special flavors of home.
